replies.author: @forevertj
replies.text: @nigeldenning5810 Adding it all together—$270 million (base production) + $50 million (estimated reshoots) + $150 million (estimated marketing)—the total cost to make and market Snow White likely falls around $470 million. If we lean toward higher-end estimates ($300 million production with reshoots + $200 million marketing), it could approach $500 million to $520 million. The New York Times cited a figure of "at least $350 million" for production and marketing combined, but this seems low given the scope of reshoots and Disney’s promotional scale. A range of $450 million to $500 million is a reasonable ballpark, reflecting the film’s troubled production history and the studio’s investment in its release.

replies.author: @kevinarnold7957

And then there is post and marketing with normal movies costing 100 M, this was more.  Then the theaters get half and the production company gets half.  So, let’s keep it simple for your simple mind.  Let’s say you made a movie for 100 M and then marketing cost 100 M, which equals 200 M.  You would need it to make 400 M to simply break even, because you only get 1/2 of the movie from the theaters.  So, even your claim of SW only costing 250 M is absurd to think this is not a flop.

author: @Altered-Perception321
text:
The Original Snow White 1937 (Wiki):

Snow White premiered at the Carthay Circle Theatre in Los Angeles, California, on December 21, 1937, and went into general release in the United States on February 4, 1938. Despite initial doubts from the film industry, it was a critical and commercial success, with international earnings of more than $8 million during its initial release against a $1.5 million production cost, becoming the highest-grossing film of 1938, and briefly holding the record of the highest-grossing sound film of all time. It was also the highest-grossing animated film for 55 years. The popularity of the film has led to its being re-released theatrically many times, until its home video release in the 1990s. Adjusted for inflation, it is one of the top-ten performers at the North American box office and is still the highest-grossing animated film with an adjusted gross of $2,297,000,000.[4] Worldwide, its inflation-adjusted earnings top the animation list.[5]

Remaking this beloved classic should be an easy home run; If you stay loyal to the source material. Idiots!

author: @keenansullivan2380
text: Since studios often lie about how much films actually cost, this film will make the flops of yesteryear that nearly destroyed studios and careers seem cheap by comparison. Adjusted for inflation, 1980's Heaven's Gate cost $170 million. That film nearly destroyed United Artists. Adjusted, 1982's One from the Heart, Francis Ford Coppola's odd musical romantic drama cost $77 million. It took Coppola a decade to crawl out from under that debt. 1963's Cleopatra cost a whopping $400 million adjusted. It took Fox over a decade to recoup costs. The media tries to claim Snow White cost $200 million. No way. Not with all the reshoots and special effects shots that were also inevitably re-tooled (As is Disney tradition). This is going to be one of the biggest box office flops of all time.
